draft.introduction: Psalm 95 calls Israel to worship but warns against hardening the heart. Hebrews 3–4 builds its entire theology of “rest” on this psalm’s reference to Meribah.

translation.text: O come, let us sing to the Lord; let us make a joyful noise to the rock of our salvation. Let us come into his presence with thanksgiving. For the Lord is a great God, and a great King above all gods.

For he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture. Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts, as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness, where your fathers put me to the test.
translation.original_text: ὅτι αὐτός ἐστιν ὁ θεὸς ἡμῶν

translator_notes.note: The word “today” (σήμερον) carries enormous weight in Hebrews, where it is repeated to argue that God’s invitation to “rest” remains perpetually open. The eschatological “today” collapses past and future into present decision.
